---
title: "CodeTrain Instructor Handbook"
subtitle: "Guidance for Richard & Joshua delivering 16-week curriculum"
author: "Speedykom Group GmbH"
date: today
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 2
    theme: cosmo
    css: styles.css
  pdf:
    documentclass: scrartcl
    papersize: letter
    geometry: margin=0.75in
    fontsize: 10pt
    number-sections: true
execute:
  eval: false
  echo: false
---

## Welcome

This handbook supports the Codetrain instructorsin delivering the 16-week CodeTrain AI curriculum to 65+ students across weekday, weekend, and online cohorts. It contains:

- Common student struggles per phase
- Recommended interventions
- Demo Day logistics
- Cohort-specific adaptations
- Ghana-context examples

---

## Phase 1: Foundations (Weeks 1–4)

### Learning Goals
- Students transition from JavaScript to Pythonic thinking
- Master variables, functions, scope, OOP
- Complete Gate 1: Python mastery

### Common Struggles
**"What's the difference between `x = [1, 2]` and `x = (1, 2)`?"**

- Students confuse list (mutable) vs tuple (immutable)
- **Intervention:** Use street analogy: "List is like a mutable marketplace stall (you can rearrange goods); tuple is like an immutable store sign (you can't change it)"
- Have them predict: "Will this code work?" before running

**"I don't understand scope (LEGB rule)"**

- JavaScript has function scope; Python adds enclosing scope (tricky for JS devs)
- **Intervention:** Draw boxes: Global box → Enclosing box → Local box. Visual helps.
- Use the notebook's LEGB examples; don't rush.

**"List comprehensions feel like magic"**

- Students write loops instead: `result = []; for x in items: result.append(x*2)`
- **Intervention:** Show them the 1-to-1 mapping: `[x*2 for x in items]` = loop without intermediate list
- Practice: Give them 5 loops; ask them to rewrite as comprehensions

### Session Structure
1. **Live code-along** (50 min): Demonstrate the concept, type slowly, make mistakes
2. **Guided practice** (20 min): Students code while you watch (share screen on weekday)
3. **Exercise time** (10 min): Assign 2–3 exercises; walk around or monitor chat

### Ghana Context to Inject
- Kwame, Ama, Yaw, Akosua as character names (familiar)
- MTN subscriber manager, MoMo transactions, Accra market data
- "Store receipts" analogy for dictionaries (list of (item, price) tuples)

### Pacing Notes
- **Tight timeline:** 4 weeks for 3 notebooks + exercises
- If stuck on scope: assign extra Scope Patrol exercises (in notebook 2)
- Do NOT skip list comprehensions (it's needed for Pandas)

### Gate 1 Check (End of Week 4)
Watch for: "Do students write `[x for x in items if x > 5]` naturally?"

- If yes → Pass Gate 1
- If no → Assign extra comprehension drills before Mini Demo Day 1

---

## Phase 2: Analytics (Weeks 5–8)

### Learning Goals
- Load, explore, clean, visualize data
- Master `.loc[]` and `.iloc[]` indexing
- Complete Gate 2: Pandas fluency

### Common Struggles
**"`.loc[]` vs `.iloc[]` – which one do I use?"**

- **Intervention:** Create a cheat sheet they can print:
  - `.loc[]` = label (column name, like dictionary key)
  - `.iloc[]` = integer position (0, 1, 2...)
- Have them predict: "Will `df.loc[0]` get the first row?" (No! 0 is a label, not position)

**"My dataset has 1M rows – Pandas is slow"**

- Real issue, not their fault
- **Intervention:** Teach `.sample(10000)` to work with subset; use `eval: false` for large operations

**"Visualization overwhelm – so many plot types"**

- **Intervention:** Decision tree: "Is it time series? → Line plot. Two variables? → Scatter. Distribution? → Histogram."
- Focus on 3 types: line, scatter, histogram (bar comes later)

### Ghana Data Examples
- Use `house_rentals.csv` throughout (real Ghana housing data)
- Discuss real price ranges in Accra/Kumasi
- Explain why cleaning matters: "Bad data → bad predictions → bad decisions for homebuyers"

### Gate 2 Check (End of Week 8)
Students should:

- [ ] Use `.loc[mask, col]` without syntax lookup
- [ ] Answer "How many transactions per user?" with `groupby().size()`
- [ ] Create 3-panel visualizations (subplots)

---

## Phase 3: Intelligence (Weeks 9–12)

### Learning Goals
- Train, evaluate, interpret ML models
- Understand precision vs recall trade-offs
- Complete Gate 3: Model building competency

### Common Struggles
**"My train/test split leads to different results each time"**

- They didn't set `random_state`
- **Intervention:** "Always set `random_state=42` for reproducibility. It's not magic—it just freezes randomness."

**"Random Forest has 1000 parameters – how do I know what to change?"**

- Overwhelm is real
- **Intervention:** Teach the "Big 3": `n_estimators` (more trees = slower but better), `max_depth` (control overfitting), `random_state`
- Leave GridSearchCV for later cohorts if they struggle

**"I'm getting 95% accuracy but my teacher says it's wrong"**

- Likely data leakage (fit on test data) or class imbalance
- **Intervention:** Ask: "Did you `.fit()` before seeing test data?" and "Are 95% of your samples one class?"

### Ghana Context
- Ghana housing prices: show feature importance (bedrooms >> color)
- MoMo fraud detection: explain precision vs recall (false positives cost customer trust)
- Cocoa yield prediction: show how ML helps smallholders

---

## Phase 4: Advanced AI (Weeks 13–16)

### Learning Goals
- Build neural networks in PyTorch
- Understand transfer learning
- Complete capstone project

### GPU/Colab Setup
- **DO:** Send Colab link before Week 13 (students need time to set up)
- **DO:** Require students to test Colab access in Week 12
- **DON'T:** Assume they know how to use Colab (show them step-by-step)
- Backup: Provide pre-trained model checkpoints for offline work

### Common Struggles
**"GPU is out of memory"**

- Likely: batch size too large or model too big
- **Intervention:** Start with small batch (32), small model (2 conv layers)

**"My loss isn't decreasing"**

- Learning rate wrong, or model not seeing data
- **Intervention:** Try `lr = 0.001` first (very conservative). Print batch loss every 10 iterations.

**"Transfer learning – do I freeze weights?"**

- Yes, at first. Then unfreeze for fine-tuning.
- **Intervention:** Provide their notebook with boilerplate code (don't make them write from scratch)

---

## Demo Day Logistics

### Mini Demo Days 1–3 (15 min per student)
**Setup:**

- 10 min: Student presents
- 5 min: Q&A
- Score in real-time using rubric

**Student Prep:**

- Send project link 24 hrs before
- Download/test their code
- Note any issues to ask about

**Your Role:**

- Ask 2–3 probing questions (not gotcha questions)
- Praise effort; be constructive on feedback
- Take a screenshot of top 3 projects to share with next cohort

### Final Demo Day (20 min per team)
**Setup:**

- 15 min: Team presents
- 5 min: Q&A
- Use full rubric (50 points)

**Team Dynamics:**

- Ask each member one question individually
- Score each person separately (fairness)
- Note any free-rider behavior for feedback

---

## Cohort-Specific Guidance

### Weekday Cohort (Mon/Wed/Fri afternoons)
- **Audience:** Working professionals, tired after work
- **Pacing:** Slightly slower (give breaks)
- **Homework:** Expect less time investment (2 hrs/wk max)
- **Tip:** Record sessions; students replay at own pace

### Weekend Cohort (Saturday mornings)
- **Audience:** Energetic; dedicated time
- **Pacing:** Can go deeper; 4-hour sessions allow immersion
- **Homework:** Weekend coding projects (Capstone can be more ambitious)
- **Tip:** Use Sunday office hours to catch up stragglers

### Online Cohort (Async + live Q&A)
- **Audience:** Distributed, variable time zones
- **Pacing:** Self-paced videos; uniform live Q&A
- **Homework:** Video submissions (code-along recordings)
- **Tip:** Use office hours to build community (many feel isolated)

---

## Office Hours & 1:1 Support

### When to Escalate to 1:1
- Student scores <15 on a Demo Day
- Student is "stuck" (can't do loop/function/indexing)
- Student missed 2+ weeks
- Student is disengaged (quiet, not submitting)

### 1:1 Session Structure (30 min)
1. **Diagnose** (5 min): "What's the blocker?"
2. **Teach** (15 min): Whiteboard; walk through concept
3. **Practice** (8 min): Have them code while you watch
4. **Plan** (2 min): What to practice before next class?

### Resources to Offer
- Links to tutorials (Real Python, freeCodeCamp)
- Related exercises from their notebook
- Peer study buddy matching

---

## Ghana Context Tips

### Cultural Relevance
- Use student names: Kwame, Ama, Yaw, Akosua, Kofi, Abena
- Reference Accra, Kumasi, Tema, Cape Coast (students' hometowns)
- Use GHS (Ghana Cedis) for all monetary examples
- Reference MTN, mPharma, Hubtel (local companies students know)

### Problem Framing
- "Why is this relevant?" before every unit
- Connect to local problems: crop disease, student retention, financial inclusion
- Celebrate when students solve problems that matter in Ghana

### Capstone Ideas (Student Suggestions)
- Cocoa yield prediction (help farmers)
- Student dropout early warning (keep students in school)
- Mobile money fraud detection (trust in mPesa/MTN)
- Electricity load forecasting (help Accra avoid blackouts)
- Crop disease detection (smartphone app for farmers)

---

## FAQs (Answers for Your Use)

**Q: A student hasn't done anything for 2 weeks. What do I do?**

- Reach out 1:1: "Haven't seen you – everything OK?"
- Offer to catch up synchronously
- Check if it's technical (no internet) or personal (life event)
- Celebrate if they return

**Q: Can I change the pacing for my cohort?**

- Yes! Use `delivery_schedule.qmd` as a template
- If students are ahead: add stretch projects (deploy to web, submit to Kaggle)
- If students are behind: extend phase by 1 week, compress next phase

**Q: What if we don't have enough time for all 4 phases?**

- Prioritize Phase 2 (Pandas) – it's the foundation for everything
- Compress Phase 4 – focus on one deep learning project vs multiple exercises
- Students can self-study Phase 4 after course ends

**Q: How do I know if a student is actually learning?**

- Can they explain concepts in their own words (not copy-paste)?
- Do they ask good debugging questions?
- Can they solve a variant of an exercise (not just replicate)?

---

## Your Success as an Instructor

You are successful when:

- ✅ >80% pass Gate 1 (Python mastery, Week 4)
- ✅ >80% pass Gate 2 (Pandas fluency, Week 8)
- ✅ >80% pass Gate 3 (Model building, Week 12)
- ✅ >70% complete capstone with portfolio-ready code
- ✅ Students can explain their code to a non-technical friend
- ✅ Students teach each other (peer learning happens)

---

## Before You Start

- [ ] Read `curriculum_strategy.qmd` (understand the big picture)
- [ ] Read `delivery_schedule.qmd` (your week-by-week roadmap)
- [ ] Review all exercise notebooks (you should solve them first!)
- [ ] Set up your Colab + test GPU access
- [ ] Create a Slack/Discord for student Q&A
- [ ] Print rubrics and post in office

---

## During the 16 Weeks

- [ ] Give feedback on exercises within 48 hours
- [ ] Celebrate Demo Days (take photos, share with future cohorts)
- [ ] Adjust pacing weekly based on student progress
- [ ] Collect 1–2 student stories of "aha!" moments to motivate future cohorts

---

## After the 16 Weeks

- [ ] Collect student feedback (what worked, what didn't)
- [ ] Identify students for mentorship/jobs at CodeTrain
- [ ] Archive top capstone projects as examples
- [ ] Share lessons learned with Ricky for next cohort curriculum improvements

---

*You've got this. Teaching Python to MERN developers is unique—you're their bridge to a new domain. Lean on the "why" (the notebooks) and the "how" (demonstrations). Good luck!*
